name: Atlantic City
short_description: Historic mining town site on BLM land offering free recreational gold and quartz collecting. High elevation (7814 ft) location with natural and weathered specimens in stream deposits.
description:
Atlantic City is a historic mining location in the Wind River Mountains of western Wyoming, situated at 7814 feet elevation on BLM-managed public land. This former gold rush settlement area remains accessible to rockhounds seeking placer gold and quartz specimens in relatively undisturbed terrain. The site offers free personal use collecting with minimal restrictions, making it an excellent destination for both beginners and experienced prospectors interested in gold history and natural mineral deposits.

The geology of Atlantic City reflects the complex metamorphic and granitic rocks of the Wind River Range. Granite and metamorphic basement rocks containing quartz veins and feldspar-rich formations were uplifted and exposed by Laramide orogeny during the Cretaceous to early Tertiary. Weathering and erosion over millions of years have concentrated gold particles and quartz fragments in stream deposits and weathered outcrops. The South Pass mining district, which includes Atlantic City, produced significant placer and lode gold historically, with recorded mining activity dating back to the 1860s.

Expect to find small gold flakes and fine dust in stream gravels, quartz pebbles of varying sizes in washes, and weathered feldspar fragments on hillsides. The high elevation and mountain setting mean cooler temperatures year-round and substantial snow cover in winter months. Access is via rough mountain roads suitable for high-clearance vehicles, with parking at the town site or along forest service roads nearby.

This location is best visited during the warmer months when roads are clear and weather permits backcountry exploration. The remoteness and elevation demand proper preparation, including adequate water, sun protection, and knowledge of changing mountain weather patterns. Rockhounders should respect the historic nature of the site and follow all BLM collection guidelines strictly.
history: Atlantic City sits within the Wind River Mountains, where Archean-age metamorphic and granitic rocks were uplifted during the Laramide orogeny. Gold mineralization occurred in quartz veins and as disseminated particles throughout granitic intrusions, with secondary concentration in stream gravels through glaciation and weathering. The area was heavily prospected and mined during the 1860s-1890s gold rush period, with both placer and lode operations active in the South Pass mining district.
tips: Search stream gravels and wash deposits for small gold flakes using a gold pan or classifier - concentrate material from low points in the creek bed where fine particles settle. Look for quartz outcrops on hillsides and weathered granite; white quartz pebbles in stream gravels often accompany gold. Bring a classifier or snuffer bottle to efficiently process material, as gold here typically occurs as fine dust and small flakes rather than coarse nuggets. The high elevation means morning starts are essential to maximize daylight hours during shorter seasons.
terrain_notes: Rugged high-altitude terrain at 7814 feet elevation with steep mountain slopes, sparse vegetation (alpine and subalpine forest), and numerous stream drainages. Granite outcrops and weathered rock fragments are common. Walking distances from parking range from 0.5 to 3 miles depending on target area. Footing is uneven with loose rock, talus slopes, and stream crossings. The terrain becomes snow-covered and impassable November through May.
best_season: June - September. High elevation snow blocks access October through May. Summer provides optimal weather, clear roads, and accessible terrain, though afternoon thunderstorms are common in July and August.
written_directions: From the town of Lander (approximately 30 miles south), take WY-131 north toward the South Pass area. Continue on WY-131 as it becomes a gravel road. Approximately 20 miles from Lander, turn onto the Atlantic City Road (marked directional signs for Atlantic City). Follow this rough mountain road for approximately 8 miles to reach the Atlantic City site. The final 5 miles are rough, high-clearance roads with stream crossings. Park near the historic town site or along wide pullouts on the road. GPS coordinates will be essential as signage is minimal.
road_conditions: Very rough mountain roads, high clearance required, seasonal washouts, stream crossings, snow and mud blockages October - May. Not recommended for passenger vehicles.
parking_notes: Limited informal parking at or near the Atlantic City town site. Additional pullouts available along the access road. No designated parking lot. Capacity is minimal, parking is unimproved earth/gravel.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or heavy equipment. Do not disturb historic structures or mining artifacts. Collect specimens of quartz, gold, and feldspar only from loose material and weathered surfaces; do not break or hammer living rock.
rules:
Personal use only
25 lbs maximum per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No historic site disturbance
Collect from loose material only
nearest_services: Lander, Wyoming (approximately 30 miles south) has gas, restaurants, lodging, and grocery stores. South Pass City (approximately 15 miles south) offers limited supplies and historical information.
accessibility_notes: Very challenging terrain. High elevation (7814 ft) causes altitude effects. Rough, uneven rocky terrain with loose talus, steep slopes, and stream crossings. No wheelchair access. Sturdy boots, excellent physical conditioning, and mountain hiking experience required. Not suitable for anyone with limited mobility or altitude sensitivity. Road access via high-clearance vehicle only.

faq.question: How do I find gold at Atlantic City, Wyoming rockhounding site?
faq.answer: Gold at Atlantic City occurs as fine flakes and dust in stream gravels and weathered granite. Use a gold pan or classifier to process material from stream beds, focusing on low spots where fine particles settle. Small quartz pebbles often accompany gold deposits. The historic mining district concentrated gold in quartz veins, so look near old mine tailings and exposed granite outcrops.

faq.question: Is a permit required to collect at Atlantic City, Wyoming?
faq.answer: No permit is required to rockhound at Atlantic City. It is located on BLM public land where personal recreational collecting is allowed without permit. However, you must follow FLPMA rules: personal use only, maximum 25 lbs per day, no commercial collection, and no motorized excavation.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound near Atlantic City, Wyoming?
faq.answer: June through September is the best season for Atlantic City rockhounding. High elevation (7814 feet) means heavy snow blocks access from October through May. Even during summer, afternoon thunderstorms are common in July and August. Early morning starts maximize daylight hours during the shorter season.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for Atlantic City gold panning?
faq.answer: Bring a 14-16 inch gold pan, classifier, or snuffer bottle for processing stream material efficiently. Pack high-clearance vehicle suitable for rough mountain roads. Essential supplies include plenty of water (no reliable water at site), sun protection, warm layers (high elevation stays cool), and a headlamp. A GPS device is highly recommended due to minimal signage.

faq.question: Is Atlantic City, Wyoming rockhounding suitable for beginners?
faq.answer: Atlantic City is not beginner-friendly. The site requires high-clearance 4x4 or AWD vehicles, excellent physical conditioning for high-altitude hiking, and significant backcountry experience. Elevation is 7814 feet, terrain is rugged with loose rock and steep slopes, and cell service is nonexistent. Beginners should gain experience at lower-elevation, more accessible Wyoming sites first.

faq.question: What gemstones besides gold can I find at Atlantic City, Wyoming?
faq.answer: Besides gold, you can collect quartz and feldspar at Atlantic City. White and clear quartz pebbles appear throughout stream gravels and weathered granite outcrops. Feldspar fragments are common in decomposed granite. These minerals accompany gold deposits and are useful for understanding the local geology of the Wind River Mountains.

meta_description: Find gold, quartz, and feldspar at Atlantic City, Wyoming on free BLM land. Historic mining site, 7814 ft elevation, personal use only. Expert rockhounders only.
